label: Opening Hook
text: Welcome to IB DP Chemistry Structure 3. Today we unlock the logic inside the periodic table, so that you can predict chemical behaviour instead of memorising it. Why does fluorine steal electrons while francium gives them away, even though both sit at the edge of the same table? By the end of this lesson, you will know the single principle behind every answer.

label: The Periodic Table as a Map
text: Think of the periodic table as a navigation map. Rows, called periods, tell you how many electron shells an atom has. Columns, called groups, tell you how many valence electrons it has. Elements in the same group behave alike because their outermost electron count is the same. Try hovering over any element in the interactive table to see its position, its atomic number, and its atomic radius.

label: Effective Nuclear Charge
text: Every trend you will see today comes from one invisible quantity, the effective nuclear charge, written as Z effective. It is the real pull felt by an outer electron. We calculate it as Z, the total protons, minus S, the shielding from inner electrons. Across a period Z effective grows sharply, because protons are added while shielding barely changes. Down a group, Z effective grows only slightly, because each new shell adds extra shielding.

label: Trend 1 — Atomic Radius
text: Atomic radius shrinks across a period and expands down a group. Across a period, stronger Z effective pulls the same shell in tighter. Down a group, each new electron shell adds a whole layer of space, outweighing the modest rise in nuclear charge. Note one subtle exception, gallium is slightly smaller than aluminium, because the newly filled 3d sub-shell shields poorly and leaves 4p electrons feeling a stronger pull.

label: Trend 2 — Ionization Energy
text: First ionization energy is the minimum energy needed to remove one electron from a gaseous atom. It rises across a period and falls down a group, the opposite of atomic radius, because a smaller atom with higher Z effective holds its outer electron more tightly. Watch for two anomalies in period three. Aluminium is slightly lower than magnesium, because its outer electron sits in a less stable 3p sub-shell. Sulfur is slightly lower than phosphorus, because pairing two electrons in the same 3p orbital costs energy.

label: Trends 3 to 5 — Electronegativity, Electron Affinity, Ionic Radius
text: Electronegativity measures the pull an atom has on electrons it shares in a bond. It increases across a period and decreases down a group, peaking at fluorine. Electron affinity is the energy released when an atom gains an electron, following a similar pattern with some quirks for nitrogen and the noble gases. Ionic radius tells a two-part story. Cations are always smaller than their parent atom, anions are always larger, and within an isoelectronic series, the species with the most protons is the smallest.

label: Predicting Chemical Behaviour
text: Now let's use the trends to predict real chemistry. Potassium reacts with water more violently than sodium, because its outer electron is farther from the nucleus and easier to lose. Fluorine is a stronger oxidising agent than chlorine, because its smaller size and higher electronegativity make it hungrier for electrons. Once you see position, you can often predict reactivity without memorising any reaction equation.

label: The One Principle and Next Steps
text: Every trend in this unit traces back to one idea. Effective nuclear charge plus distance from the nucleus determines how tightly the outer electron is held, and that single balance explains size, ionisation energy, electronegativity, and reactivity. Take the self-check quiz to confirm your mastery, then move on to Structure 3 point 2, ionic bonding, where these trends decide which elements give up electrons and which ones take them.
